item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really fine work, here. Love the title reference, and I think you wrapped up the article nicely. Very enjoyable read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I used the Spanish spelling alphabet found here for the code names. Other than the obvious América/nukes connection, there isn't meant to be any symbolism on those names.

It's possible that it was a mistake to put symbolism into the first codename and none of the rest, but my intention was that was a joke made by the 4400 Researchers that allowed them a level of plausible deniability — "It wasn't our fault, that was simply what the code name had to be!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it a lot, as I always like SCP's based in mesoamerican stuff (you know my people's stuff :P), the only nitpick I have is why one of the effects of the SCP is called "Barcelona"? Like everything in the article has names pertaining to Mexico except this which is a Spaniard city (and a football team but that's besides the point) or is there any reason for this? I re-read the article several times but couldn't make a connection.

+1 anyway though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its a good SCP but in my opinion you should be more specific about almost everything. I liked it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I went to Palenque some times when i was a child. I still remember the legends that surrounds that place.
This was a nice read. I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While fascinating, the idea of someone being able to make nuclear bombs through alchemy and so on is pretty frightening. Granted, they'd have to be skillful, but it does mean that nuclear weapons could become portable and thus, more dangerous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely one of my favorite articles on here. The Oppenheimer line absolutely blew me away, and the frescos depicting symptoms of radiation poisoning was such a clever touch! I love the idea that, despite the fact that they were given to humanity by a demon, they're still just nukes. +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the Mayans made some very effective warning signs huh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like how this one plays with a bunch of different ideas. Had a lot of fun with it.

+ Spoilers, kinda?
- Sojourner, huh?

I got a serious kick out of seeing the Serpent showing up. Probably more than was warranted, considering it isn't really the focus of the story, but it was good fun all the same. I really like how that was integrated into the story: it wasn't the focus or the point, just making use of an established element of the universe to fulfill a necessary function, signposted for people who are familiar with it without bogging things down for people who aren't. Having it outright use a synonym for the word "wanderer" was a lot more effective than I would have expected.

I'm kinda surprised there aren't more works that connect it to Mesoamerican cultural context. 1203 is the only other one I'm aware of, but I may have missed something.

Anyways, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay i love this so so much. Reading about ancient Mayan thaumonuclear bombs (good pun) is a great way to spend my study hall where I'm supposed to be studying for Spanish but it's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just want to say that I have seen this crit and have been too busy to look into it and make an alternative I feel comfortable with. When I have more time, I will edit the article with a better measure of radiation. In the meantime, I would just handwave that they took the measurements when those standards were in place, and haven't gone back to measure it after 1965? That's still bad, I know, but something.

Interesting. I had an inkling from the name, and the frescos pointed it out nicely that this was an ancient nuclear waste dump or the like being rediscovered in the modern age the same way we may fear some post-civilisation-collapse successors might find our own (ok, it's bombs in this case, but whatever), and it was generally nicely written.

I will say that SCP-4400 does contain a small amount of nuclear waste, which isn't mentioned that much heavily in the SCP because it isn't viewed as important as the actual bombs. It's also there, but of little-to-no value (it's not even anomalous!) and is overshadowed by the bombs.

SCP-4400-América is the collective designation for four eigenweapons and the waste products of their construction, which were evidently created around 650 CE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. I had an inkling from the name, and the frescos pointed it out nicely that this was an ancient nuclear waste dump or the like being rediscovered in the modern age the same way we may fear some post-civilisation-collapse successors might find our own (ok, it's bombs in this case, but whatever), and it was generally nicely written.

Tiny point of order though. Having spent a couple years, rather too long ago, actually working up close and personal with radioactive materials, I can't say I've ever had any real contact with Roentgens. I have no idea whether 250R is a massively dangerous amount of background radiation, or an artificially low one. I tried googling a few terms including "roentgens background radiation" and got nowhere. It's an archaic term for the rather poorly defined metric of radiation "exposure", it was never adopted by the SI, and simply nobody uses it any more - and hardly anyone uses its derived and historically more useful units like the Rem - because it's been superceded. As far as my brief research can figure out, this happened before I was born.

If the Foundation is supposed to use proper SI units for all its measurements, or at least any kind of customary and familiar ones, then Roentgens are entirely out of place and almost as anachronistic as the Palenque Nuclear Waste Dump itself. Like I said, it's a tiny thing, but it broke immersion for me. And I still don't know if it's supposed to indicate a high background radiation, and, if so, by how much.

If you just want a readout of the background radiation? It's Becquerels. And generally you don't need to get scared until the meter hits a few tens or hundreds of kBq, or a few MBq if you're just passing through. Hundreds of MBq or any form of GBq is when it's time to turn and run without looking back. At least, that's how I remember it, I might be off by a magnitude or three, take with a pinch of salt and do your own checking.

Instantaneous dose rate (which governs short term radiation sickness)? Grays. Can't say I touched those much, as in that particular line of work if you even had a reason to calculate them then it meant things had already climbed into the handbasket to hell.

Equivalent absorbed dose rate (the thing that really matters for cumulative exposure)? Sieverts. Of which about 1 mSv is typical background radiation, and depending on your line of work your annual dose limit above and beyond that is either 1, 20 or 50mSv; 8Sv, especially short term, is guaranteed death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But even if they were to go off, so what? All that'd happen is a bunch of empty buildings in an uninhabited pocket dimension would be destroyed. So long as nobody else steals them they're no threat at all and, were someone inclined to steal nukes, there are more powerful ones such a person could go after that don't require you to know about the hidden pocket dimension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really nice. It's a rich beautifully crafted description of a location, these guys did a lot better job of reliable warning signs than the "menacing earthworks" or "we genetically engineer cats to glow in the dark and start a religion with the tenet of staying away from glowing cats" (??) crowd, and I like the neat little bow on the end that they're not particularly terrible nukes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same.

I liked this. I liked that it ultimately wasn't a world-ender, any more than current world-enders. I like this because it's Safe. I really, really like how the bombs worked and the reveal at the end. (Also, yes, the end was melodramatic, but melodramatic in a way that felt like people talking.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I already know of such a demon, and his name is Robert Oppenheimer.

Loved this line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of transmitting the primal fear of death to communicate the danger and wrongness of nuclear weapons to future visitors! Since this is a familiar concept handled very well, this is now a favorite of mine. Have a well-earned upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The intended structure of this article is that there is a slow rise, with each addendum increasing the threat and power of the nuclear bombs, reaching a peak with the demon calling them the fruits of the gods, and the most powerful weapons ever. The twist, therefore, is going in the opposite direction and taking the stance that nuclear bombs are not really something to write home about.

I understand if that doesn't work for you, but I did want to explain what I intended the "twist" to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole direction of the story was "straight, when you thought it would zag." It went on for far too long, leading me to the expectation that there must be some interesting, shocking twist. It turned out that the twist was… these were exactly what they seemed to be. It's well-written and has a great deal of very nice imagery, but it's not enough to overcome the weakness of the overall narrative. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow I LOVED this! I recognized the title immediately and got the chills I always get when I see that line. Its such a good take on how our modern attempts to seal away radioactive dangers could be seen by future investigators, and you captured it well within the Foundation format. I hope this wins 4000!
+1 (I'd do many more if I could)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can find one (1) thing that I'd change that isn't a matter of ~*style*~; the symptoms depicted in Fresco #0001 are also consistent with the modern concept of radiation poisoning, and that consistency seems really notable to me here.

I've modified that to "perception" to be more accurate with what I was trying to convey.

I'm struck by the reverent tone Researcher Balenciaga takes in his SDR; certainly it's a dream worth revering, I guess, and I guess he's a man who respects that.

I try to write characters with distinct personalities — in this case, Balenciaga is a man who respects the paranormal and the spiritual. Surely not everybody in the Foundation operates with the same cold clinical efficiency, and there have to be some supernatural souls who are recruited.

item.title: Scooby Doo: Nuclear Edition
item.content:encoded:
My post title reflects a little how I felt reading this: a bunch of build up, things getting spookier, and then the mask is pulled away to reveal it was Nuclear Fission all along. And they would've gotten away with it too if it hadn't been for you damn meddling kids trained agents.

Humor aside, I really like this, mostly because I think it fills a niche that "realistically" (as much as SCPs can be realistic) the Foundation would have encountered more. Here's a thing that was created a long time ago through anomalous means. It was groundbreaking and terrifying and unheard of at the time, a truly world-altering item of unprecedented power. By today's standards it's just *kind of* powerful, and we already know how to contain it. No no, don't get me wrong, the original creators did a bang-up job of making sure we were all scared as shit getting in here, but really there's not much for us to fret over, comparatively speaking. Just wall it off, put some guards on it, and make sure nobody finds out about the thing that shouldn't. Good? Good. Alright, who wants lunch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew I was gonna upvote this when I saw the title.

This is perfect in fitting with the contest theme, and you took a possibly boring idea ("what if the anomaly is NOT ANOMALOUS? :O") and just ran with it in a way that was enthralling. Good job, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't figure out why I downvoted this initially. I fixed that.

I can find one (1) thing that I'd change that isn't a matter of ~*style*~; the symptoms depicted in Fresco #0001 are also consistent with the modern concept of radiation poisoning, and that consistency seems really notable to me here.

I can't even really speak on style; the technical material is excellent, and the dialogue and narrated text is either from non-native speakers or translated from Spanish or Yucatec, and is therefore allowed to be slightly odd.

I'm struck by the reverent tone Researcher Balenciaga takes in his SDR; certainly it's a dream worth revering, I guess, and I guess he's a man who respects that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nitpick:

+ show block
– hide block

A thaumaturgic consultant assigned to Mobile Task Force Xi-Kai analyzed the tablet and concluded that it was instructions for a ritual to create and restrict a specific non-corporeal ontokinetic entity.

Wouldn't this person just be a thaumatologist? Thaumatology being the common term on the site for the science of magic.

I like it. The Mayans using divine powers to acquire mundane WMDs is a creative direction to go. Additionally, the description of the frescos was quite good, being both vivid and interesting, keeping me going to find out what the deal with the place was.

That said, the reference to the Pattern Screamers was a tad ham-handed, I feel. I personally don't know how you could go about making it more subtle or believable, but the fact that this entity basically refers to them by name kills the immersion a tad.

Still, a solid article. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In counterpoint to those who don't like the end dialogue, I really enjoyed it as the cap to the article. This site is Safe, it's extradimensional (keeping others safe from accidents) and anybody who would want to steal them is better off making a mundane weapon. It's really inspired and a great example for newbies of how anomaly classes work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was entertaining to read and the formating is so nice that it makes it even more enjoyable.

Making that image was no big deal but I'm glad you liked it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed with previous posters; the ending dialogue is a bit heavy handed, but also undersells the threat of anomalous nukes. That's going to be a hard one to cover up.
But the idea of the Foundation classifying nukes as Safe (which despite their lethality is broadly reasonable, especially in an isolated area) and an excellent take on the current thinking about Yucca Mountain gives this a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the conversation at the end undersells the danger. Like, yeah, they're just nukes, and not even particularly powerful ones, but they're nukes that can't be disarmed and are probably pretty fragile.

The status quo seems stable enough, but messing with the set up at all strikes me as having a good chance of accidentally detonating them. Which may not be apocalyptic, but it would still be pretty bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"These personnel are to be stocked and trained with of Class B amnestics." I think you accidentally a word.

I really like this, and I saw the connection to "how to warn people 10,000 years in the future about the nuclear waste we're burying now" before I read the author's note. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is relatively simple and mundane, but hell, I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ancient Mayan nukes? I'll take it. That said, the procedures and description could use some tightening.

Excavation attempts at digging into SCP-4000

This passage, for example, is worded redundantly, and indicative of what I see that could use more attention.

SCP-4000-δ

This is first mentioned in the dream addendum (sidenote: I'm glad they include this but don't give a lot of weight to it, i.e., include it as a definite part of the overall anomaly), but I don't think it was ever defined?

That final addendum though, I really like it. That's getting my upvote right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Response
– hide block

Is there any reason for these designations, instead of, say, -1, -2, and an MTF number? This isn't a criticism, mind you, I just thought these were unusual choices, and I can't see the logic behind them.

Concerning the subdesignations: The primary reason (which applies both in universe and out) is that codenames are easier to remember and distinguish than numbers.

In the earliest stages of this draft, the subdesignations were Greek letters, which is something that both I and other authors have done before. However, since this SCP is set in Mexico, and all the characters are either Hispanic or Mayan,1 I didn't like the Greek characters and wanted to use something different. I settled on a Spanish phonetic dictionary, which I felt was appropriate.

Yes, it's an unusual choice, but it's something I chose to give it a little more spice, and also based on a general desire to have things be more immediately recognizable.

As for Xi-Kai, that one wasn't my decision: that MTF was created by MaliceAforethought and used in SCP-2668 and SCP-3716. I can't say why he chose that designation. I considered crosslinking to one of those, but ended up choosing not to - I still might.

In addition to missing a word, surely the number of rings in the diagram would narrow the range of possibilities greatly?

I've since clarified that section to really show what I mean by it — basically, while it is a representation of the Bohr model, it's not a very good representation of the Bohr model. The number of valence rings clearly doesn't match up with the depicted size of the nucleus, the electrons are missing, etc.

Those would probably be found at a bomb construction site, rather than at a detonation. Nuclear fallout is made up of a whole mess of different radioactive materials.

I'm not the most knowledgeable about the exact composition of nuclear fallout, but those two isotopes were chosen in part because their half-lives were long enough that there'd still be some left 1,300 years after an explosion, and because they are used in the construction of a nuclear bomb. (Also because Cyantreuse recommended them.) If you have other isotopes that could possibly be found, please suggest them!

EDIT: Cyan posted while I was writing this response, I've added a mention that other isotopes formed from the decay chain of U235 were also found.

A good response. Beyond the anomalous use of a subcritical mass, there is little danger here. See the "Summary Report of the NTH Country Experiment" for more info.

Pretty much. 4000-América is a rather small collection of nuclear bombs, and none of them are even that big.

Footnotes
1. Except Dr. McCusick, who is a reference to a real woman who worked on the Manhattan project

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Words can't express how pumped I am for these nuclear-themed 4000 entries. Excited +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those would probably be found at a bomb construction site, rather than at a detonation. Nuclear fallout is made up of a whole mess of different radioactive materials.

I'm the one that suggested that line be changed to what it is now, my reasoning being that the radioisotopes U-235 and Pu-239, used in most atomic bombs (atomic, not hydrogen) stay around for quite a while even after detonation, given that U-235 has a half-life of 703.8 million years and Pu-239 has a half-life of 24,100 years. These isotopes are present both at construction sites (but not to a strong degree, or else someone's violating the local health physicist's wishes) and after detonation.

That said, for U-235, it would be reasonable to also throw in some detections of other long-lived isotopes from its decay chain, given how much time has passed and the fact that a lot of fissioning has already happened (and in accordance with your accurate statement that fallout is "a whole mess of different radioactive materials").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, looks good, but I do have questions. Warning: long and possibly nitpicky.

+ Collapsed for length
– hide block

SCP-4000-América
Mobile Task Force Xi-Kai
SCP-4000-Barcelona
EoI-4000-Cáceres

Is there any reason for these designations, instead of, say, -1, -2, and an MTF number? This isn't a criticism, mind you, I just thought these were unusual choices, and I can't see the logic behind them.

Fresco #0010 to be an anachronistic depiction of a modern atom under the Bohr model, although the nature of the diagram prevents conclusive identification of what element is being depicted.

In addition to missing a word, surely the number of rings in the diagram would narrow the range of possibilities greatly?

However, they did note traces of plutonium-239 and uranium-235 isotopes in the soil.

Those would probably be found at a bomb construction site, rather than at a detonation. Nuclear fallout is made up of a whole mess of different radioactive materials.

Dir. Osorio: Cáceres? Cáceres is a demon that will teach you how to split an atom. I already know of such a demon, and his name is Robert Oppenheimer.

A good response. Beyond the anomalous use of a subcritical mass, there is little danger here. See the "Summary Report of the NTH Country Experiment" for more info.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite cool and the ending is great, particularly with the reveal that the ancient nuclear bombs are just that - nuclear bombs. Although the ending dialogue is somewhat heavy-handed, I appreciate that it is at least delivered as a dialogue with active participants rather than as a monologuing note at the end.

item.title: Nuclear bombs are Safe.
item.content:encoded:
A while ago, I read an article about how the US government was planning it's long-term radioactive waste dump sites. In particular, what caught my attention was the talk about how they planned to show that the materials are dangerous. Since the radioactive danger will outlast the English language, they have to do so in a way that doesn't use language, and as such they did everything they could to make the places as scary and filled with death iconography as possible.
EDIT: The article I was thinking of is called This is not a place of honor.

I would like to thank DrChandra, PeppersGhost, spikebrennan, Cyantreuse, Kakroom, Djoric, JackalRelated, LordStonefish Ihp, UraniumEmpire, Captain Kirby and Dave from IRC (who is apparently Eskobar) for looking this over before posting.

The nuclear blood serpents are for CadaverCommander

Hun, Ka'a and Óox are Yucatec Mayan for one, two and three. This is a language which still has fluent and native speakers. América, Barcelona and Cáceres are from the Spanish phonetic alphabet — it was NATO at one point, but I decided that Spanish made more sense.

Attributions:

  • archaeology.png is the work of Tat0b910
    • Really, a special shoutout to Tatob here. I dropped an awful pixel art version of the logo into #site19, and Tatob volunteered to create the logo here instead.
  • The MTF and Administrative logos are, I believe, the work of Osobist from the Russian site.
    • They were clearly created for the Russian site and therefore released under a CC license.
  • The other two images (DSC_0360 and DSC_0351) are my own work, and hereby released under CC BY-SA 3.0
